title: The Philosophical Works of Descartes
description: The Philosophical Works of Descartes — knowledge fragments for AI agents

memes.label: About This Wiki
memes.description: A knowledge wiki for "Philosophical Works" — this page is auto-generated from book metadata
'Table of Contents'

memes.label: Animal
memes.description: Descartes' doctrine of animals as mindless automata — the beast-machine hypothesis.

memes.label: Anselm of Canterbury
memes.description: Anselm of Canterbury (1033–1109), Benedictine monk, Archbishop of Canterbury, and originator of the ontological argument for God's existence.

memes.label: Antoine Arnauld
memes.description: Antoine Arnauld (1612–1694), French theologian and philosopher whose Fourth Objections to Descartes' Meditations raised the Cartesian Circle problem and whose Port-Royal Logic (1662, with Pierre Nicole) became the most influential logic textbook of the early modern period — a systematic fusion of Cartesian epistemology with the art of reasoning.

memes.label: Thomas Aquinas
memes.description: Thomas Aquinas (1225–1274), Dominican philosopher-theologian whose synthesis of Aristotelian philosophy and Christian theology established the scholastic framework that Descartes' philosophy was designed to replace.

memes.label: Aristotelianism
memes.description: The philosophical tradition based on Aristotle's works, dominant in European universities through the 17th century — Descartes' primary foil and the intellectual establishment he sought to replace.

memes.label: Aristotle
memes.description: Ancient Greek philosopher (384–322 BCE), student of Plato and founder of the Peripatetic school, whose natural philosophy and metaphysics dominated Western thought through the scholastic tradition — both Descartes' principal foil and an acknowledged source of his mechanistic principles.

memes.label: Atomism
memes.description: The doctrine that the physical world consists of indivisible particles (atoms) moving in void — a precursor to Cartesian mechanism that Descartes both acknowledges and transforms.

memes.label: Attribute
memes.description: In Cartesian metaphysics, an attribute is the essential property constituting a substance's nature — thought for mind, extension for body.

memes.label: Augustine of Hippo
memes.description: Augustine of Hippo (354–430), early Christian theologian whose anticipation of the cogito — "Si fallor, sum" (If I am deceived, I am) — and whose treatment of divine illumination, free will, and the problem of error provided Descartes with a theological framework he both inherited and transformed.

memes.label: Axiom
memes.description: The nature and role of axioms in Descartes' philosophy — self-evident propositions that serve as first principles of knowledge, grounded as eternal truths in the human mind.

memes.label: George Berkeley
memes.description: George Berkeley (1685–1753), Irish immaterialist philosopher whose radical claim that only minds and ideas exist — *esse est percipi* (to be is to be perceived) — represents one of the most extreme and logically coherent developments from Cartesian premises about ideas and perception.

memes.label: Bertrand Russell
memes.description: Bertrand Russell (1872–1970), British philosopher and logician, was one of the most influential figures of 20th-century analytic philosophy — his widely read *History of Western Philosophy* (1945) shaped modern understanding of Descartes as the "father of modern philosophy," and his logical-analytic method defined the tradition against which continental Cartesian scholarship defined itself.

memes.label: Blaise Pascal
memes.description: Blaise Pascal (1623–1662), French mathematician, physicist, and religious thinker — Descartes' most formidable French contemporary, whose mathematical genius matched Descartes' own and whose critique of Cartesian rationalism from a religious-existential standpoint (*Pensées*) offers the most profound alternative to the Cartesian project of grounding faith in reason.

memes.label: Body
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, extended substance (res extensa) whose essential nature is extension in length, breadth, and depth — the subject of local motion, figure, and all corporeal properties.

memes.label: The Cartesian Circle
memes.description: A synthetic analysis of the Cartesian Circle — the charge that Descartes' argument for the reliability of clear and distinct ideas depends on God's existence, which itself is proved using clear and distinct ideas.

memes.label: Cartesianism
memes.description: Cartesianism — the philosophical movement founded by René Descartes and developed by his followers in France, the Netherlands, and beyond; characterized by mind-body dualism, mechanistic physics, the method of systematic doubt, and the grounding of knowledge in the cogito.

memes.label: Cause
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, the metaphysical principle that the cause must contain at least as much reality (perfection) as the effect — the engine of the causal argument for God's existence and a cornerstone of Cartesian physics.

memes.label: Certainty
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, the subjective correlate of truth — the state of the mind when it perceives clearly and distinctly, such that doubt is psychologically impossible and rationally unwarranted.

memes.label: "Appendix"
memes.description: "Reasons Which Establish the Existence of God, and the Distinction Between the Mind and Body of Man, Disposed in Geometrical Order (From the Reply to the Second Objections—Latin, 1670, pp."

memes.label: "Prefatory Note by the Author"
memes.description: "If this Discourse appear too long to be read at once, it may be divided into six Parts: and, in the first, will be found various considerations touching the Sciences; in the second, the principal..."

memes.label: "Part I"
memes.description: "Good sense is, of all things among men, the most equally distributed; for everyone thinks himself so abundantly provided with it, that those even who are the most difficult to satisfy in everything..."

memes.label: "Part II"
memes.description: "I was then in Germany, attracted thither by the wars in that country, which have not yet been brought to a termination; and as I was returning to the army from the coronation of the emperor, the..."

memes.label: "Part III"
memes.description: "And finally, as it is not enough, before commencing to rebuild the house in which we live, that it be pulled down, and materials and builders provided, or that we engage in the work ourselves,..."

memes.label: "Part IV"
memes.description: "I am in doubt as to the propriety of making my first meditations in the place above mentioned matter of discourse; for these are so metaphysical, and so uncommon, as not, perhaps, to be acceptable to..."

memes.label: "Part V"
memes.description: "I would here willingly have proceeded to exhibit the whole chain of truths which I deduced from these primary; but as with a view to this it would have been necessary now to treat of many questions..."

memes.label: "Part VI"
memes.description: "Three years have now elapsed since I finished the treatise containing all these matters; and I was beginning to revise it, with the view to put it into the hands of a printer, when I learned that..."

memes.label: "Meditations on the First Philosophy"
memes.description: "In Which the Existence of God and the Immortality of the Soul Are Demonstrated"

memes.label: "Dedication"
memes.description: "To the Very Sage and Illustrious the Dean and Doctors of the Sacred Faculty of Theology of Paris."

memes.label: "Preface to the Reader"
memes.description: "I have already slightly touched upon the questions respecting the existence of God and the nature of the human soul, in the “Discourse on the Method of rightly conducting the Reason, and seeking..."

memes.label: "Synopsis of the Six Following Meditations"
memes.description: "In the First Meditation I expound the grounds on which we may doubt in general of all things, and especially of material objects, so long at least, as we have no other foundations for the sciences..."

memes.label: "Letter of the Author to the French Translator of the Principles of Philosophy Serving for a Preface"
memes.description: "Sir,—The version of my Principles which you have been at pains to make, is so elegant and finished as to lead me to expect that the work will be more generally read in French than in Latin, and..."

memes.label: "Dedication"
memes.description: "To the Most Serene Princess, Elizabeth, Eldest Daughter of Frederick, King of Bohemia, Count Palatine, and Elector of the Sacred Roman Empire."

memes.label: "I"
memes.description: "That in order to seek truth, it is necessary once in the course of our life, to doubt, as far as possible, of all things."

memes.label: "II"
memes.description: "That we ought also to consider as false all that is doubtful."

memes.label: "III"
memes.description: "That we ought not meanwhile to make use of doubt in the conduct of life."

memes.label: "IV"
memes.description: "Why we may doubt of sensible things."

memes.label: "V"
memes.description: "Why we may also doubt of mathematical demonstrations."

memes.label: "XVIII"
memes.description: "How the prejudice of an absolute vacuum is to be corrected."

memes.label: "XIX"
memes.description: "That this confirms what was said of rarefaction."

memes.label: "XX"
memes.description: "That from this the nonexistence of atoms may likewise be demonstrated."

memes.label: "XXI"
memes.description: "It is thus also demonstrated that the extension of the world is indefinite."

memes.label: "XXII"
memes.description: "It also follows that the matter of the heavens and earth is the same, and that there cannot be a plurality of worlds."

memes.label: "XXIII"
memes.description: "That all the variety of matter, or the diversity of its forms, depends on motion."

memes.label: "XXIV"
memes.description: "What motion is, taking the term in its common use."

memes.label: "XXV"
memes.description: "What motion is properly so called."

memes.label: "I"
memes.description: "That we cannot think too highly of the works of God."

memes.label: "VI"
memes.description: "That we possess a free will, by which we can withhold our assent from what is doubtful, and thus avoid error."

memes.label: "II"
memes.description: "That we ought to beware lest, in our presumption, we imagine that the ends which God proposed to himself in the creation of the world are understood by us."

memes.label: "III"
memes.description: "In what sense it may be said that all things were created for the sake of man."

memes.label: "CLXXXVIII"
memes.description: "Of what is to be borrowed from disquisitions on animals and man to advance the knowledge of material objects."

memes.label: "CLXXXIX"
memes.description: "What perception (*sensus*) is, and how we perceive."

memes.label: "CXC"
memes.description: "Of the distinction of the senses; and, first, of the internal, that is, of the affections of the mind (passions), and the natural appetites."

memes.label: "CXCI"
memes.description: "Of the external senses; and first of touch."

memes.label: "CXCII"
memes.description: "Of taste."

memes.label: "CXCIII"
memes.description: "Of smell."

memes.label: "CXCIV"
memes.description: "Of hearing."

memes.label: "VII"
memes.description: "That we cannot doubt of our existence while we doubt, and that this is the first knowledge we acquire when we philosophize in order."

memes.label: "CXCV"
memes.description: "Of sight."

memes.label: "CXCVI"
memes.description: "That the soul perceives only in so far as it is in the brain."

memes.label: "CXCVII"
memes.description: "That the nature of the mind is such that from the motion alone of body the various sensations can be excited in it."

memes.label: "CXCVIII"
memes.description: "That by our senses we know nothing of external objects beyond their figure [or situation], magnitude, and motion."

memes.label: "CXCIX"
memes.description: "That there is no phenomenon of nature whose explanation has been omitted in this treatise."

memes.label: "CC"
memes.description: "That this treatise contains no principles which are not universally received; and that this philosophy is not new, but of all others the most ancient and common."

memes.label: "CCI"
memes.description: "That sensible bodies are composed of insensible particles."

memes.label: "CCII"
memes.description: "That the philosophy of democritus is not less different from ours than from the common.41 But it may be said that Democritus also supposed certain corpuscles that were of various figures, sizes, and..."

memes.label: "CCIII"
memes.description: "How we may arrive at the knowledge of the figures, [magnitudes], and motions of the insensible particles of bodies."

memes.label: "CCIV"
memes.description: "That, touching the things which our senses do not perceive, it is sufficient to explain how they can be, [and that this is all that aristotle has essayed]."

memes.label: "VIII"
memes.description: "That we hence discover the distinction between the mind and the body, or between a thinking and corporeal thing."

memes.label: "CCV"
memes.description: "That nevertheless there is a moral certainty that all the things of this world are such as has been here shown they may be."

memes.label: "CCVI"
memes.description: "That we possess even more than a moral certainty of it."

memes.label: "CCVII"
memes.description: "That, however, I submit all my opinions to the authority of the church."

memes.label: "IX"
memes.description: "What thought (*cogitatio*) is."

memes.label: "X"
memes.description: "That the notions which are simplest and self-evident, are obscured by logical definitions; and that such are not to be reckoned among the cognitions acquired by study, [but as born with us]."

memes.label: "XI"
memes.description: "How we can know our mind more clearly than our body."

memes.label: "XII"
memes.description: "How it happens that every one does not come equally to know this."

memes.label: "XIII"
memes.description: "In what sense the knowledge of other things depends upon the knowledge of God."

memes.label: "XIV"
memes.description: "That we may validly infer the existence of God from necessary existence being comprised in the concept we have of him."

memes.label: "XV"
memes.description: "That necessary existence is not in the same way comprised in the notions which we have of other things, but merely contingent existence."

memes.label: "XVI"
memes.description: "That prejudices hinder many from clearly knowing the necessity of the existence of God."

memes.label: "XVII"
memes.description: "That the greater objective (representative) perfection there is in our idea of a thing, the greater also must be the perfection of its cause."

memes.label: "XVIII"
memes.description: "That the existence of God may be again inferred from the above."

memes.label: "XIX"
memes.description: "That, although we may not comprehend the nature of God, there is yet nothing which we know so clearly as his perfections."

memes.label: "XX"
memes.description: "That we are not the cause of ourselves, but that this is God, and consequently that there is a God."

memes.label: "XXI"
memes.description: "That the duration alone of our life is sufficient to demonstrate the existence of God."

memes.label: "XXII"
memes.description: "That in knowing the existence of God, in the manner here explained, we likewise know all his attributes, as far as they can be known by the natural light alone."

memes.label: "XXIII"
memes.description: "That God is not corporeal, and does not perceive by means of senses as we do, or will the evil of sin."

memes.label: "XXIV"
memes.description: "That in passing from the knowledge of God to the knowledge of the creatures, it is necessary to remember that our understanding is finite, and the power of God infinite."

memes.label: "XXV"
memes.description: "That we must believe all that God has revealed, although it may surpass the reach of our faculties."

memes.label: "XXVI"
memes.description: "That it is not needful to enter into disputes22 regarding the infinite, but merely to hold all that in which we can find no limits as indefinite, such as the extension of the world, the divisibility..."

memes.label: "XXVII"
memes.description: "What difference there is between the indefinite and the infinite."

memes.label: "XXVIII"
memes.description: "That we must examine, not the final, but the efficient, causes of created things."

memes.label: "XXIX"
memes.description: "That God is not the cause of our errors."

memes.label: "XXX"
memes.description: "That consequently all which we clearly perceive is true, and that we are thus delivered from the doubts above proposed."

memes.label: "XXXI"
memes.description: "That our errors are, in respect of God, merely negations, but, in respect of ourselves, privations."

memes.label: "XXXII"
memes.description: "That there are only two modes of thinking in us, viz., the perception of the understanding and the action of the will."

memes.label: "XXXIII"
memes.description: "That we never err unless when we judge of something which we do not sufficiently apprehend."

memes.label: "XXXIV"
memes.description: "That the will as well as the understanding is required for judging."

memes.label: "XXXV"
memes.description: "That the will is of greater extension than the understanding, and is thus the source of our errors."

memes.label: "XXXVI"
memes.description: "That our errors cannot be imputed to God."

memes.label: "XXXVII"
memes.description: "That the chief perfection of man is his being able to act freely or by will, and that it is this which renders him worthy of praise or blame."

memes.label: "XXXVIII"
memes.description: "That error is a defect in our mode of acting, not in our nature; and that the faults of their subjects may be frequently attributed to other masters, but never to God."

memes.label: "XXXIX"
memes.description: "That the liberty of our will is self-evident."

memes.label: "XL"
memes.description: "That it is likewise certain that God has foreordained all things."

memes.label: "XLI"
memes.description: "How the freedom of our will may be reconciled with the divine preordination."

memes.label: "XLII"
memes.description: "How, although we never will to err, it is nevertheless by our will that we do err."

memes.label: "XLIII"
memes.description: "That we shall never err if we give our assent only to what we clearly and distinctly perceive."

memes.label: "XLIV"
memes.description: "That we uniformly judge improperly when we assent to what we do not clearly perceive, although our judgment may chance to be true; and that it is frequently our memory which deceives us by leading us..."

memes.label: "XLV"
memes.description: "What constitutes clear and distinct perception."

memes.label: "XLVI"
memes.description: "It is shown, from the example of pain, that a perception may be clear without being distinct, but that it cannot be distinct unless it is clear."

memes.label: "XLVII"
memes.description: "That, to correct the prejudices of our early years, we must consider what is clear in each of our simple28 notions."

memes.label: "XLVIII"
memes.description: "That all the objects of our knowledge are to be regarded either (1) as things or the affections of things: or (2) as eternal truths; with the enumeration of things."

memes.label: "XLIX"
memes.description: "That the eternal truths cannot be thus enumerated, but that this is not necessary."

memes.label: "L"
memes.description: "That these truths are clearly perceived, but not equally by all men, on account of prejudices."

memes.label: "LI"
memes.description: "What substance is, and that the term is not applicable to God and the creatures in the same sense."

memes.label: "LII"
memes.description: "That the term is applicable univocally to the mind and the body, and how substance itself is known."

memes.label: "LIII"
memes.description: "That of every substance there is one principal attribute, as thinking of the mind, extension of the body."

memes.label: "LIV"
memes.description: "How we may have clear and distinct notions of the substance which thinks, of that which is corporeal, and of God."

memes.label: "LV"
memes.description: "How duration, order, and number may be also distinctly conceived."

memes.label: "LVI"
memes.description: "What are modes, qualities, attributes."

memes.label: "LVII"
memes.description: "That some attributes exist in the things to which they are attributed, and others only in our thought; and what duration and time are."

memes.label: "LVIII"
memes.description: "That number and all universals are only modes of thought."

memes.label: "LIX"
memes.description: "How universals are formed; and what are the five common, viz., genus, species, difference, property, and accident."

memes.label: "LX"
memes.description: "Of distinctions; and first of the real."

memes.label: "LXI"
memes.description: "Of the modal distinction."

memes.label: "LXII"
memes.description: "Of the distinction of reason (logical distinction)."

memes.label: "LXIII"
memes.description: "How thought and extension may be distinctly known, as constituting, the one the nature of mind, the other that of body."

memes.label: "LXIV"
memes.description: "How these may likewise be distinctly conceived as modes of substance."

memes.label: "LXV"
memes.description: "How we may likewise know their modes."

memes.label: "LXVI"
memes.description: "How our sensations, affections, and appetites may be clearly known, although we are frequently wrong in our judgments regarding them."

memes.label: "LXVII"
memes.description: "That we are frequently deceived in our judgments regarding pain itself."

memes.label: "LXVIII"
memes.description: "How in these things what we clearly conceive is to be distinguished from that in which we may be deceived."

memes.label: "LXIX"
memes.description: "That magnitude, figure, etc., are known far differently from colour, pain, etc."

memes.label: "LXX"
memes.description: "That we may judge of sensible things in two ways, by the one of which we avoid error, by the other fall into it."

memes.label: "LXXI"
memes.description: "That the chief cause of our errors is to be found in the prejudices of our childhood."

memes.label: "LXXII"
memes.description: "That the second cause of our errors is that we cannot forget these prejudices."

memes.label: "LXXIII"
memes.description: "The third cause is, that we become fatigued by attending to those objects which are not present to the senses; and that we are thus accustomed to judge of these not from present perception but from..."

memes.label: "LXXIV"
memes.description: "The fourth source of our errors is, that we attach our thoughts to words which do not express them with accuracy."

memes.label: "LXXV"
memes.description: "Summary of what must be observed in order to philosophize correctly."

memes.label: "LXXVI"
memes.description: "That we ought to prefer the divine authority to our perception:34 but that, apart from things revealed, we ought to assent to nothing that we do not clearly apprehend."

memes.label: "I"
memes.description: "The grounds on which the existence of material things may be known with certainty."

memes.label: "II"
memes.description: "How we likewise know that the human body is closely connected with the mind."

memes.label: "III"
memes.description: "That the perceptions of the senses do not teach us what is in reality in things, but what is beneficial of hurtful to the composite whole of mind and body."

memes.label: "IV"
memes.description: "That the nature of body consists not in weight, hardness, colour and the like, but in extension alone."

memes.label: "V"
memes.description: "That the truth regarding the nature of body is obscured by the opinions respecting rarefaction and a vacuum with which we are preoccupied."

memes.label: "VI"
memes.description: "In what way rarefaction takes place."

memes.label: "VII"
memes.description: "That rarefaction cannot be intelligibly explained unless in the way here proposed."

memes.label: "VIII"
memes.description: "That quantity and number differ only in thought (*ratione*) from that which has quantity and is numbered."

memes.label: "IX"
memes.description: "That corporeal substance, when distinguished from its quantity, is confusedly conceived as something incorporeal."

memes.label: "X"
memes.description: "What space or internal place is."

memes.label: "XI"
memes.description: "How space is not in reality different from corporeal substance."

memes.label: "XII"
memes.description: "How space differs from body in our mode of conceiving it."

memes.label: "XIII"
memes.description: "What external place is."

memes.label: "XIV"
memes.description: "Wherein place and space differ."

memes.label: "XV"
memes.description: "How external place is rightly taken for the superficies of the surrounding body."

memes.label: "XVI"
memes.description: "That a vacuum or space in which there is absolutely no body is repugnant to reason."

memes.label: "XVII"
memes.description: "That a vacuum in the ordinary use of the term does not exclude all body."

memes.label: Cartesian Circle
memes.description: The problem of circular reasoning in Descartes' argument for the reliability of clear and distinct perception.

memes.label: Clear and Distinct Perception
memes.description: Descartes' criterion of truth — that whatever is perceived clearly and distinctly cannot be false — which serves as the foundation of his epistemology and the guarantee of all certain knowledge.

memes.label: Cogito
memes.description: Descartes' first indubitable principle — "I think, therefore I am" (*cogito ergo sum*) — the Archimedean point from which all Cartesian knowledge is rebuilt after methodical doubt.

memes.label: Common Notion
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, self-evident propositions innate to the mind — such as "equals added to equals give equals" — that serve as axioms for all demonstration and are known with certainty when attentively considered.

memes.label: Index of Philosophical Concepts
memes.description: A comprehensive index of all philosophical concepts documented in this wiki, dynamically rendered from concept-type entries.

memes.label: Confidence
memes.description: The subjective certainty or trust accompanying belief — Descartes analyzes both its legitimate epistemic role and its dangers when excessive. (004-014)

memes.label: Consciousness (Conscientia)
memes.description: Descartes' concept of consciousness as the mind's immediate awareness of its own thoughts — the foundation of the cogito.

memes.label: Conservation (Self-Conservation)
memes.description: In Descartes' metaphysics, the doctrine that the continued existence of any finite substance requires the same infinite power as its original creation — a power that only God possesses.

memes.label: Contingency
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, contingency refers to the mode of existence belonging to finite things — existence that is possible but not necessary — contrasted with the necessary existence that belongs to God alone.

memes.label: Creation
memes.description: Descartes' doctrine that God continuously creates and conserves the world at every moment, grounding all existence and motion in divine action.

memes.label: Deduction
memes.description: The logical process of deriving conclusions from premises through chains of reasoning — central to Descartes' rationalist method and his vision of unified scientific knowledge. (005-011)

memes.label: Definition
memes.description: Descartes' critique of scholastic definitions and his alternative approach based on clear and distinct ideas.

memes.label: Democritus
memes.description: Ancient Greek atomist who explained natural phenomena through corpuscles — acknowledged by Descartes as a precursor to mechanism but distinguished from Cartesian physics by indivisible atoms and the void.

memes.label: Demonstration
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, the method of deriving conclusions from self-evident first principles by a chain of clear and distinct inferences — modeled on geometrical proof and foundational to his vision of unified science.

memes.label: Desire
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, desire (désir) is one of the six primitive passions — an agitation of the soul caused by the animal spirits that moves the will toward a future good represented by the mind as attainable.

memes.label: Discourse on the Method
memes.description: Descartes' 1637 methodological manifesto and intellectual autobiography, originally published as the preface to three scientific essays — the founding text of modern philosophy that introduces the method of doubt and the cogito argument.

memes.label: Divine Attributes
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, the divine attributes are the perfections belonging to God as an infinitely perfect substance — including infinity, eternity, immutability, omniscience, and omnipotence — known through the natural light of reason and essential to Cartesian metaphysics.

memes.label: Doubt
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, the methodological instrument by which all beliefs are subjected to systematic scrutiny — the engine of the Meditations that clears away uncertain opinions to uncover indubitable foundations for knowledge.

memes.label: Dream Argument
memes.description: The dream argument is Descartes' skeptical hypothesis that there are no certain marks by which waking experience can be distinguished from dreaming, used in Meditation I and Discourse IV to undermine confidence in sensory knowledge.

memes.label: Dualism
memes.description: Descartes' doctrine that mind and body are two really distinct substances — thinking substance (*res cogitans*) and extended substance (*res extensa*) — each capable of existing without the other.

memes.label: Duration (Duratio)
memes.description: Descartes' concept of duration — the persistence of a thing in existence, distinguished from time as a mode of thought.

memes.label: Elisabeth of Bohemia
memes.description: Redirect to Princess Elisabeth of Bohemia.

memes.label: Emotion (Passion)
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, emotions (passions) are perceptions caused by the body that the soul experiences as its own states, central to his theory of mind-body interaction and moral philosophy.

memes.label: Empiricism
memes.description: Empiricism — the epistemological tradition, developed by Locke, Berkeley, and Hume, that holds all knowledge to be derived from sensory experience, rejecting the rationalist doctrine of innate ideas and arguing that the mind is a blank slate at birth.

memes.label: Epicureanism
memes.description: Ancient philosophical school combining atomistic physics with hedonistic ethics — Descartes both inherits and transforms its mechanistic physics while rejecting its ethics and theology.

memes.label: Epicurus
memes.description: Ancient Greek atomist philosopher, founder of Epicureanism — his atomism, revived through Lucretius, was part of the mechanical tradition Descartes both inherits and transforms.

memes.label: Error
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, a privation arising from the misuse of free will — when the infinite will affirms or denies beyond what the finite intellect clearly and distinctly perceives.

memes.label: Essence
memes.description: In Descartes' metaphysics, essence is what constitutes a substance's fundamental nature — thought for mind, extension for body — and is grasped through its principal attribute, upon which all other properties depend.

memes.label: Esteem
memes.description: In Descartes' moral philosophy, esteem (estime) is the passion of self-regard — a judgment of one's own worth that, when properly grounded in the recognition of free will as the sole basis of human dignity, becomes the virtue of generosity (générosité), the crown of the Cartesian ethical system.

memes.label: Eternal Truths
memes.description: Descartes' doctrine of eternal truths — necessary, immutable propositions created by God's free will, foundational to Cartesian metaphysics and science.

memes.label: Eternal Truths
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, self-evident propositions — such as "nothing comes from nothing" and the principle of non-contradiction — that have their seat in the mind and are ultimately grounded in God, who freely created them.

memes.label: Eternity
memes.description: The timeless, unchanging mode of existence attributed to God in Cartesian metaphysics — God knows and wills all things from eternity. (099-002)

memes.label: Ethics
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, the science of morals — the "last degree of wisdom" that presupposes knowledge of all other sciences, and which Descartes approaches through his "provisional morality" while the foundations of knowledge are being established.

memes.label: Ethics (Spinoza)
memes.description: Ethics, Demonstrated in Geometrical Order (1677), Baruch Spinoza's magnum opus — the most rigorous and radical development of Cartesian rationalism, in which Spinoza deploys Descartes' geometric method to argue for a monistic metaphysics of one infinite substance that is both God and Nature, transforming Cartesian dualism into a system of immanent necessity.

memes.label: Evil
memes.description: Descartes' understanding of evil as a privation of being, rooted in his theodicy that God is not responsible for human error or moral evil.

memes.label: Evil Demon
memes.description: 'The "malin génie" or evil demon hypothesis in Descartes'' First Meditation — a radical skeptical scenario in which an all-powerful deceiver systematically falsifies all of one''s beliefs, driving the method of doubt to its extreme limit. (013-001)'

memes.label: Existence
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, the fundamental actuality of a substance or thing — distinguished from essence, and central to the ontological argument for God's existence, where necessary existence belongs to God's perfection.

memes.label: Experience
memes.description: In Descartes' methodology, experience (experientia) provides the raw material for reason to analyze — valuable for testing hypotheses and amassing data, but insufficient alone for achieving certain knowledge.

memes.label: Experiment
memes.description: The systematic observation and testing of natural phenomena — a necessary complement to rational deduction in Descartes' scientific methodology. (009-002)

memes.label: Extension
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, the principal attribute of corporeal substance — that by which body occupies space in length, breadth, and depth, and the defining characteristic that distinguishes body from mind.

memes.label: Fear
memes.description: In Descartes' theory of the passions, fear (crainte) is the anticipation of a future evil, distinguished from terror by its moderate intensity and from hope by its orientation toward a negative outcome.

memes.label: Pierre de Fermat
memes.description: Pierre de Fermat (1607–1665), French mathematician and jurist, was Descartes' greatest mathematical rival — their dispute over the method of tangents, mediated by Marin Mersenne, exposed the tension between Descartes' analytic geometry and Fermat's algebraic approach, a rivalry that drove innovation in 17th-century mathematics.

memes.label: First Principles
memes.description: 'The foundational truths from which all certain knowledge is derived in Descartes'' philosophical system — the indubitable starting points established by the method of doubt, including the cogito, the existence of God, and the reliability of clear and distinct perception.'

memes.label: Foundationalism
memes.description: Descartes' epistemological doctrine that all knowledge must rest on secure, indubitable foundations — self-evident first principles from which further truths are deduced — making him the archetypal foundationalist in Western philosophy.

memes.label: Francis Bacon
memes.description: Francis Bacon (1561–1626), English philosopher and statesman whose empiricist method of induction from systematic observation stands in defining contrast to Descartes' rationalist deduction from first principles — together they frame the rationalist-empiricist divide that structures early modern philosophy.

memes.label: Freedom
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, freedom (liberty, free will) is the power of the will to affirm or deny, pursue or avoid, without external constraint — it is the most perfect human faculty, infinite in scope like God's will, and the source of both human dignity and human error.

memes.label: Galileo Galilei
memes.description: Galileo Galilei (1564–1642), Italian natural philosopher whose mathematical-experimental method and heliocentric astronomy shaped Descartes' conception of science — and whose condemnation by the Church profoundly influenced Descartes' caution about publishing his own cosmological views.

memes.label: Pierre Gassendi
memes.description: Pierre Gassendi (1592–1655), French priest, astronomer, and philosopher who revived Epicurean atomism and authored the Fifth Objections to Descartes' Meditations.

memes.label: Geometry
memes.description: The role of geometry in Descartes' philosophy as the model of certain knowledge, the paradigm of clear and distinct reasoning, and the inspiration for his philosophical method.

memes.label: Geometry (Descartes)
memes.description: One of the three scientific essays appended to the Discourse on the Method — Descartes' revolutionary treatise that founded analytic geometry by applying algebra to geometric problems.

memes.label: God
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, the infinite, supremely perfect substance whose existence is demonstrated through causal and ontological arguments, and who serves as the ultimate guarantor of clear and distinct perception.

memes.label: The Good
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, the good (bonum) is identified with perfection and ultimately with God as the source of all goodness. The will naturally pursues what the intellect presents as good, error arises from pursuing apparent goods without clear knowledge, and the highest human good consists in the right use of reason and free will.

memes.label: Gravity
memes.description: In Descartes' natural philosophy, the phenomenon of gravity in terrestrial bodies, which he rejects as a real quality inherent in matter and instead explains mechanistically through the motion of subtle particles.

memes.label: Habit (Consuetudo)
memes.description: Descartes' concept of habit as the acquired disposition of the mind — both an obstacle to clear thinking and a tool for methodical training.

memes.label: Thomas Hobbes
memes.description: Thomas Hobbes (1588–1679), English materialist philosopher whose Objections to Descartes' Meditations (1641) — the first and most trenchant external critique — challenged the inference from cogito to immaterial substance and defended a thoroughgoing materialism against Cartesian dualism.

memes.label: Hope
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, hope (espoir) is a passion of the soul arising from the representation of a future good as possible but uncertain — a blend of desire (for the object) and joy (at its perceived attainability) that motivates action while exposing the mind to the risk of disappointment when its grounds are ill-judged.

memes.label: David Hume
memes.description: David Hume (1711–1776), Scottish empiricist philosopher whose skeptical critique of causation, the self, and natural theology posed the most formidable challenge to Cartesian rationalism and awakened Kant from his "dogmatic slumber."

memes.label: Idea
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, the form of any thought by the immediate perception of which one is conscious of that thought — the fundamental unit of mental content through which all knowledge is mediated.

memes.label: Imagination
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, imagination (imaginatio) is a mode of thinking limited to material objects — a faculty that forms mental images of extended, figured, and movable things, distinct from pure intellection and dependent on the body.

memes.label: Immortality of the Soul
memes.description: The Cartesian doctrine that the human soul, as an immaterial thinking substance distinct from the body, is naturally immortal — its existence does not depend on the body and it therefore survives bodily death.

memes.label: Infinity
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, the idea of the infinite (infinity) is primarily associated with God — an innate idea of an all-perfect being whose positive infinity cannot be grasped by the finite human mind but is nonetheless perceived clearly as prior to and grounding the idea of the finite.

memes.label: Innate Idea
memes.description: 'A concept or truth present in the mind from birth, not derived from sensory experience — central to Descartes'' rationalist epistemology and his argument for the existence of God. (013-003)'

memes.label: Intellect
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, the intellect (understanding) is the faculty of clear and distinct perception — finite in scope, passive in operation — which, together with the will, constitutes the mind's two fundamental powers.

memes.label: Intuition
memes.description: 'In Cartesian philosophy, the direct, non-discursive apprehension of truth by the mind alone — the fundamental act of knowing that grounds all deductive reasoning. (013-002)'

memes.label: Isaac Beeckman
memes.description: Isaac Beeckman (1588–1637), Dutch natural philosopher and mathematician, was the first intellectual mentor of the young Descartes — their chance meeting in Breda in 1618 awakened Descartes' mathematical vocation, and Beeckman's mechanistic-causal physics profoundly shaped the development of Cartesian natural philosophy.

memes.label: Joy
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, joy (laetitia, joie) is one of the six primitive passions — the emotion of the soul occasioned by the perception of a present good, involving a physiological response in which the blood dilates more readily in the heart, and distinguished from mere bodily pleasure by its intellectual dimension.

memes.label: Judgment
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, the act combining intellect and will by which the mind affirms or denies something of an idea — the locus of truth and falsity, and the point where free will and knowledge intersect.

memes.label: Immanuel Kant
memes.description: Immanuel Kant (1724–1804), German Enlightenment philosopher whose "critical philosophy" synthesized rationalism and empiricism — profoundly shaped by Descartes' turn to the subject while radically transforming its metaphysical foundations.

memes.label: Knowledge
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, certain and evident cognition grounded in clear and distinct perception — distinguished from mere opinion or belief by its indubitability and its foundation in rationally secure first principles.

memes.label: Laws of Nature
memes.description: 'In Cartesian natural philosophy, the eternal and immutable rules established by God governing all motion and change in the material world — the foundation of Descartes'' mechanistic physics.'

memes.label: Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz
memes.description: Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz (1646–1716), German rationalist philosopher and mathematician who critiqued Cartesian physics and developed a metaphysics of monads — immaterial, mind-like simple substances that constitute the ultimate reality.

memes.label: Leviathan
memes.description: Leviathan (1651), Thomas Hobbes' masterpiece of political philosophy — a comprehensive treatise on human nature, sovereignty, and the social contract that shares with Descartes a mechanistic worldview but rejects his dualism, grounding all reality, including thought, in matter in motion.
aliases: Forme and Power of a Commonwealth

memes.label: John Locke
memes.description: John Locke (1632–1704), English empiricist philosopher whose sustained critique of Cartesian innate ideas in the Essay Concerning Human Understanding established empiricism as the dominant tradition in British philosophy.

memes.label: Logic
memes.description: Descartes' critique of scholastic syllogistic logic and his proposal for a new method of discovery based on clear and distinct ideas rather than formal deduction.

memes.label: Love
memes.description: Descartes' analysis of love as a passion of the soul, culminating in the intellectual love of God.

memes.label: Lucretius
memes.description: Titus Lucretius Carus (c. 99–c. 55 BCE), Roman poet and Epicurean philosopher, author of De Rerum Natura, the most complete surviving exposition of ancient atomism.

memes.label: Nicolas Malebranche
memes.description: Nicolas Malebranche (1638–1715), French Oratorian priest and Cartesian philosopher, best known for his doctrine of occasionalism and the vision in God.

memes.label: Marin Mersenne
memes.description: Marin Mersenne (1588–1648), French Minim friar and mathematician who served as the central hub of the 17th-century philosophical correspondence network — Descartes' closest intellectual confidant, the organizer of the Objections to the Meditations, and the figure through whom the new philosophy was disseminated across Europe.

memes.label: Mathematics
memes.description: The role of mathematics in Descartes' philosophy as a model of certainty, its place in the method of doubt, and the nature of mathematical truth as grounded in eternal truths.

memes.label: Matter
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, matter (corporeal substance) is defined solely by extension — the fundamental physical substance whose essence is to occupy space, distinct from mind (thinking substance).

memes.label: Maurice of Nassau
memes.description: Maurice of Nassau (1567–1625), Prince of Orange and Stadtholder of the Dutch Republic, was the military commander under whom the young Descartes served in the army of the Dutch States — it was during this service at Breda in 1618 that Descartes met Isaac Beeckman, the encounter that awakened his mathematical and philosophical vocation.

memes.label: Mechanics
memes.description: Descartes' mechanistic conception of physics, explaining all natural phenomena through matter in motion.

memes.label: Mechanism
memes.description: Descartes' doctrine that all natural phenomena, including living bodies, can be explained solely by matter in motion — the extension, figure, and local motion of parts — without appeal to substantial forms, final causes, or occult qualities.

memes.label: Medicine
memes.description: Descartes' conception of medicine as a practical science grounded in mechanistic physics, aimed at preserving health — which he regarded as "the chief of all goods" — through knowledge of the body's machine-like operations.

memes.label: Meditations on First Philosophy
memes.description: Descartes' 1641 masterpiece of metaphysics and epistemology — six meditations that establish the cogito, prove God's existence, establish the mind-body distinction, and lay the foundations of modern philosophy.

memes.label: Memory
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, memory (memoria) is a corporeally-dependent faculty for storing and recalling past ideas — a useful instrument of thought that nevertheless constitutes a persistent source of error when confused with genuine present knowledge.

memes.label: Metaphysics
memes.description: In Descartes' system, metaphysics is "first philosophy" — the foundational discipline that establishes the existence of God and the soul, providing the unshakeable principles upon which all physics and the other sciences depend.

memes.label: Metaphysics (Aristotle)
memes.description: Aristotle's Metaphysics — the foundational text of Western metaphysics and the principal target of Descartes' rejection of scholastic Aristotelianism; Descartes' entire philosophical project can be understood as an attempt to replace Aristotle's substance-based ontology with one grounded in the thinking subject.

memes.label: Method
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, the systematic procedure for directing reason toward truth — comprising rules for analysis, synthesis, and enumeration, presented in the Discourse on the Method as the foundation of all scientific inquiry.

memes.label: Mind
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, the thinking substance (res cogitans) whose whole essence or nature consists in thinking — defined in opposition to body as the unextended subject of thought.

memes.label: Mind–Body Problem
memes.description: The philosophical problem of how an immaterial mind (or soul) interacts with a material body, arising from Descartes' real distinction argument and shaping subsequent philosophy of mind.

memes.label: Mind-Body Problem Through the Centuries
memes.description: A synthetic overview tracing the mind-body problem from Descartes' substance dualism through its critics and successors, drawing on the wiki's coverage of mind, body, dualism, substance, and related concepts.

memes.label: Mode
memes.description: In Cartesian metaphysics, modes are the determinate ways in which attributes of substance are expressed — as shape is a mode of extension, so particular ideas are modes of thought.

memes.label: Michel de Montaigne
memes.description: Michel de Montaigne (1533–1592), French essayist and sceptical philosopher whose revival of Pyrrhonian arguments created the intellectual crisis to which Descartes' method of doubt was the definitive response — the sceptic who made the *Meditations* necessary.

memes.label: Motion
memes.description: In Descartes' natural philosophy, motion is the change of a body's place relative to contiguous bodies — governed by laws derived from God's immutability and the cornerstone of mechanistic physics.

memes.label: Nature
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, "nature" operates on multiple levels — it denotes the essence of a substance (the nature of mind is thought, the nature of body is extension), the created order as a whole, and the innate cognitive faculty or "light of nature" by which we grasp first principles.

memes.label: Necessity
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, necessity (nécessité) characterizes the existence of God (who exists necessarily), the eternal truths (which are necessary because God willed them so), and the logical connections in valid deduction — but Descartes' doctrine that God freely created even the laws of logic makes necessity radically dependent on divine will.

memes.label: Isaac Newton
memes.description: Isaac Newton (1643–1727), English physicist and mathematician whose Principia Mathematica (1687) established the laws of motion and universal gravitation, displacing Cartesian vortex physics and reshaping the relationship between natural philosophy and mathematics.

memes.label: Objective Reality
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, the reality possessed by an idea in virtue of its representational content — the degree of being or perfection that an idea represents, distinct from the formal reality it has as a mode of thought.

memes.label: Order
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, order (ordo) is the rational arrangement of propositions from simplest to most complex — the second rule of method and the governing principle of both the order of knowing (ordo cognoscendi) and the order of being (ordo essendi) in his system.

memes.label: Passions
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, the perceptions or emotions of the soul caused by the movement of animal spirits in the body, which are to be understood and regulated rather than suppressed.

memes.label: Passions of the Soul
memes.description: Passions of the Soul (Les Passions de l'âme, 1649), Descartes' last major work, written for Princess Elisabeth of Bohemia — a comprehensive treatise on the emotions that treats the passions as bodily-mental unions to be understood and mastered through reason, completing the Cartesian philosophical system with an ethics grounded in the mind-body distinction.

memes.label: Perception
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, the cognitive act by which the mind apprehends ideas — ranging from sensory perception (confused) to clear and distinct intellectual perception, which serves as the criterion of truth.

memes.label: Perfection
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, the degree of reality or entity possessed by a thing — the scale by which infinite substance (God) exceeds finite substances, and finite substances exceed accidents or modes.

memes.label: Index of Philosophers
memes.description: A comprehensive index of philosophers referenced in this wiki, ordered chronologically by birth year.

memes.label: Philosophy
memes.description: In Descartes' system, philosophy is the tree of knowledge whose roots are metaphysics, trunk is physics, and branches are medicine, mechanics, and morals — all grounded in the certainty of first principles discovered by reason.

memes.label: Physics (Aristotle)
memes.description: Aristotle's foundational treatise on natural philosophy — the work that defined the framework of physics for two millennia and which Descartes' mechanistic physics explicitly aims to replace.

memes.label: Plato
memes.description: Ancient Greek philosopher, student of Socrates and teacher of Aristotle — Descartes engages with Platonic themes of innatism, the priority of intellect over sense, and the immortality of the soul.

memes.label: Platonism
memes.description: Platonism — the philosophical tradition founded by Plato, centered on the theory of Forms and the distinction between the intelligible and sensible realms; Descartes' own dualism between mind and body, and his doctrine of innate ideas, bear the deep imprint of Platonic metaphysics mediated through Augustine and the Christian Platonist tradition.

memes.label: Power (Potentia)
memes.description: Descartes' concept of power — from God's infinite creative power, to the mind's power of judgment, to the body's mechanical powers.

memes.label: Prejudice
memes.description: Preconceived opinions and sensory biases that Descartes identifies as the primary obstacle to attaining certain knowledge, to be eliminated through methodical doubt.

memes.label: Pride
memes.description: Excessive self-regard or arrogance — Descartes identifies pride as a moral failing that ancient moralists sometimes mistook for virtue. (004-010)

memes.label: Princess Elisabeth of Bohemia
memes.description: Princess Elisabeth of Bohemia (1618–1680), Descartes' most significant philosophical correspondent, known for her penetrating critiques of Cartesian mind–body dualism.

memes.label: Principles of Philosophy
memes.description: Descartes' 1644 systematic treatise presenting his complete philosophical system — from metaphysics to physics to cosmology — organized in textbook format as a replacement for Aristotelian scholastic manuals.

memes.label: Probability
memes.description: In Descartes' epistemology, a degree of plausibility distinct from certainty, which he systematically excludes from scientific knowledge but provisionally accepts for practical conduct.

memes.label: Queen Christina of Sweden
memes.description: Queen Christina of Sweden (1626–1689), the extraordinary monarch who summoned Descartes to Stockholm in 1649 to tutor her in philosophy — a decision that led to Descartes' death from pneumonia after only four months at her court, ending his life at age 53 in the service of the most remarkable philosophical patron of the age.

memes.label: Rationalism
memes.description: Rationalism — the 17th–18th century philosophical tradition, inaugurated by Descartes and developed by Spinoza and Leibniz, that holds reason to be the primary source of knowledge, grounded in innate ideas and deductive certainty rather than sensory experience.

memes.label: Reason
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, reason (bon sens/ratio) is the universal human faculty for judging correctly and distinguishing truth from error — the natural light that, when properly applied through method, leads to certain knowledge.

memes.label: René Descartes
memes.description: René Descartes (1596–1650), French philosopher and mathematician, known as the father of modern philosophy — his method of radical doubt, mind-body dualism, and mechanistic physics laid the foundations of Western rationalism and the scientific revolution.

memes.label: Rules for the Direction of the Mind
memes.description: Rules for the Direction of the Mind (Regulae ad Directionem Ingenii, c. 1628), Descartes' earliest major philosophical work — an unfinished treatise that lays the epistemological foundations of the Cartesian method, proposing rules for the guidance of the natural light of reason toward certain knowledge.
aliases: Rules for the Direction of the Mind

memes.label: Sadness (Tristitia)
memes.description: In Descartes' taxonomy of the passions, sadness (tristitia) is one of the six primitive passions — the perception of present evil that inclines the soul toward aversion and withdrawal.

memes.label: Scepticism
memes.description: Ancient school of philosophy that advocated suspension of judgment (epoché) to achieve tranquillity — the tradition Descartes borrows from and transforms through methodical doubt.

memes.label: Scholasticism
memes.description: Scholasticism — the medieval philosophical-theological tradition that synthesized Aristotelian philosophy with Christian doctrine, reaching its apex in Thomas Aquinas' Summa Theologica; this was the dominant intellectual framework that Descartes was educated in at La Flèche and that his entire philosophical project sought to overthrow.

memes.label: Index of Philosophical Schools
memes.description: An index of philosophical schools and traditions referenced across this wiki.

memes.label: Science
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, science (scientia) is certain knowledge derived from evident first principles — distinct from probable opinion and grounded in the method of mathematical reasoning applied to nature.

memes.label: Sensation
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, sensation (sensus) is a mode of thinking caused by bodily motions in the nerves and brain — a confused but practically useful mode of perception that reveals the mind-body union rather than the true nature of external things.

memes.label: Sense
memes.description: Descartes' account of sense perception — its role, limitations, and relation to pure intellection.

memes.label: Skepticism as Method
memes.description: A synthetic analysis tracing skepticism as a philosophical method — from ancient Pyrrhonian and Academic skepticism through Descartes' methodological doubt to Hume's empiricist skepticism.

memes.label: Socrates
memes.description: Ancient Greek philosopher, teacher of Plato — Descartes associates Socrates with the honest confession of ignorance and the method of rational inquiry that clears the ground for certain knowledge.

memes.label: Soul
memes.description: Descartes' conception of the rational soul as an immaterial, thinking substance distinct from body and naturally immortal.

memes.label: Space
memes.description: Descartes' doctrine that space — extension in length, breadth, and depth — is in reality identical with corporeal substance, differing from body only in our mode of conceiving it.

memes.label: Baruch Spinoza
memes.description: Baruch Spinoza (1632–1677), Dutch rationalist philosopher who built on Cartesian metaphysics to develop a monistic system in which God and Nature are one substance — the most radical and systematic successor to Descartes' philosophy.

memes.label: Stoicism
memes.description: Ancient school of philosophy emphasizing virtue, reason, and living in accordance with nature — Descartes engages with Stoic ethics and physics, particularly in his theory of the passions.

memes.label: Subject
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, the subject is the thinking self — the "I" that thinks, doubts, and exists as the foundational certainty of knowledge. It is the substance in which thought immediately resides.

memes.label: Substance
memes.description: The fundamental ontological category in Descartes' philosophy — that which exists in such a way that it needs nothing else in order to exist, whose principal attribute constitutes its essence.

memes.label: The World (Le Monde)
memes.description: Descartes' first systematic treatise on physics and cosmology, suppressed after Galileo's condemnation — the work that first developed his mechanistic physics and vortex theory.
aliases: Traité du monde et de la lumière

memes.label: Thought (Cogitatio)
memes.description: Descartes' fundamental category of mental activity — *cogitatio* encompasses all conscious operations (intellect, will, imagination, sensation) and defines the essence of the thinking substance.

memes.label: Time
memes.description: Descartes' conception of time as a mode of thought measuring duration, distinct from the divine attribute of eternity.

memes.label: Most Referenced Pages
memes.description: A cross-type ranking of the most referenced pages in this wiki, ordered by citation count.

memes.label: Truth
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, that which is clearly and distinctly perceived — guaranteed by God's veracity, and the goal of all philosophical inquiry through the method of doubt.

memes.label: Understanding
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, the understanding (intellectus/intellectio) is the faculty of perception by which the mind apprehends ideas — the passive yet foundational mode of thinking that, together with the will, constitutes the two basic operations of mind.

memes.label: Virtue
memes.description: Descartes' treatment of virtue as rooted in the knowledge of good and the firm resolution to act upon right judgment, culminating in the identification of all true virtues with wisdom.

memes.label: Void
memes.description: Descartes' doctrine that a vacuum or void space — extension without corporeal substance — is a contradiction in terms, impossible in nature because extension is identical with body.

memes.label: Will
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, the faculty of affirming or denying — infinite in scope, the locus of human freedom, and the source of error when it exceeds the intellect's clear and distinct perception.

memes.label: Wisdom
memes.description: In Descartes' philosophy, wisdom (sapientia) is the perfect knowledge of all that man can know — the ultimate goal of philosophical inquiry that consists in understanding truths through their first causes, and the highest degree of human perfection and felicity attainable by natural reason.

memes.label: Ludwig Wittgenstein
memes.description: Ludwig Wittgenstein (1889–1951), Austrian-British philosopher whose critique of the possibility of a private language — and thus of the Cartesian conception of mind as a private inner realm — represents one of the most influential anti-Cartesian arguments in 20th-century philosophy.

memes.label: Wonder (Admiration)
memes.description: In Descartes' taxonomy, wonder (admiration) is the first of the six primitive passions — a sudden surprise at a novel object that arrests attention before any judgment of its value as good or evil.

memes.label: Index of Philosophical Works
memes.description: An index of philosophical works referenced in this wiki, ordered chronologically by publication year.
